planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring; in zone 9 sow September–November or January–February. Mascara holds dark red color remarkably well in warm fall conditions that cause most red lettuces to revert toward green. Harvest outer leaves for a cut-and-come-again harvest or pull entire plants at maturity.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Mascara is a deeply red-colored romaine-type lettuce producing upright, compact heads of dark wine-red to nearly black-red leaves with the crisp, crunchy texture and pronounced midrib of romaine — combining the structural quality of cos-type lettuces with an extreme depth of red pigmentation that rivals the darkest red looseleaf varieties. The dramatic, almost black-red color maintains well through the warm fall temperatures that cause most red lettuces to lighten; Mascara's consistent dark color regardless of temperature makes it valuable for market growers who need reliable color in variable fall conditions. The flavor is mild, slightly sweet, and crisp — very similar to standard romaine in taste despite the striking color difference. In mesclun and mixed salad plantings, the very dark Mascara leaves provide the strongest color contrast against green lettuces. The intense red-black coloring indicates very high anthocyanin content and corresponding antioxidant value.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Mascara Romaine Variety Notes
University of California Cooperative Extension — Red Romaine Lettuce Varieties
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Red Romaine Types

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring; in zone 9 sow September–November for fall harvest or January–February for spring. Cimmaron is a full-sized red romaine that heads up well in zone 9 cool-season conditions. The tall, upright habit makes it efficient in raised beds. Successive sowings every 3 weeks through fall provide a continuous supply through winter.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Cimmaron is a large, full-headed red romaine lettuce from the American Southwest — its name evokes the Cimarron River of the Oklahoma-Kansas plains country. It produces tall, upright, well-formed romaine heads with bronze-red to mahogany-red outer leaves and a pale green-to-cream interior heart, combining the classic romaine flavor and crunch with deep red coloration. The large, mature heads at 58–68 days are suitable for Caesar salad in the traditional whole-leaf format, where the combination of romaine structure and visual color impact makes Cimmaron stands out among red romaine options. Cimmaron is slower-bolting than most red looseleaf types and holds quality in the garden longer as a planted head. The flavor is mild, crisp, and slightly sweet — traditional romaine quality with no bitterness. In zone 9 fall planting produces the most satisfying heads; spring-planted Cimmaron benefits from light shade cloth in late spring to delay bolting.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Cimmaron Romaine Heritage Variety
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Red Romaine Lettuce Types
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Cos and Romaine Lettuce

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before fall transplant; in zone 9 transplant September–October for a December–February harvest. Tardivo requires a forcing technique: in late fall, cut the tops of the plants to 1 inch, dig the roots, pack them tightly in boxes of moist sand or sawdust, and force in darkness at 50–60°F for 3–5 weeks. This forcing step is essential — without it, Tardivo produces standard chicory leaves rather than the distinctive elongated, curled Tardivo form. In zone 9 the mild winter allows some growers to force plants in place with dark covers.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Tardivo is the most prized and most expensive radicchio in Italy — a Treviso-type radicchio that requires a traditional forcing process in which the plants are dug after fall frosts, packed in darkness, and allowed to produce elongated, curved white-and-red leaf chicons resembling a firework spray or an open hand. The resulting forced Tardivo heads have long, narrow, spoon-shaped, curling red-and-white leaves with a dramatic, sculptural appearance that makes them a luxury vegetable in Italian restaurants and specialty markets — they are served raw in salads, grilled whole, braised, or used as striking garnishes. The bittersweet flavor is the most refined of all radicchio types, with the forcing process reducing bitterness while concentrating sweetness. Tardivo commands the highest price of any radicchio in Italian markets; in home gardens the complex forcing requirement is rewarded with a genuinely special vegetable unlike any other readily available.
sources: Slow Food Foundation — Presidia Radicchio di Treviso Tardivo
University of California Cooperative Extension — Radicchio and Chicory Forcing
Seeds of Italy — Tardivo Radicchio Growing and Forcing Notes

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before fall transplant; in zone 9 transplant September–October for a November–February harvest. Rossa di Treviso is the elongated, pointed-head variety of Treviso radicchio — distinct from the round-head Rossa di Verona and from the forced Tardivo type. It heads naturally in cool weather without the forcing process required for Tardivo, making it the more accessible Treviso-type for home growers. In zone 9 light frosts intensify the red-purple color and reduce bitterness.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Rossa di Treviso (Red Treviso Radicchio) is the elongated, cone-to-torpedo-shaped Treviso type of radicchio, producing pointed heads of 10–14 inches long with deep wine-red to burgundy leaves with prominent white midribs — visually distinct from the round, compact Rossa di Verona radicchio. The naturally-heading (non-forced) Treviso type forms its distinctive elongated shape through the cool-weather growing process without requiring the dark forcing period of Tardivo, making it accessible to home growers who want Treviso-type radicchio without the complexity of forcing. The flavor is bittersweet and complex — similar to Rossa di Verona but with the elongated form lending itself to halving and grilling (the standard preparation for Treviso-type radicchio) where the heads can be placed directly on the grill without splitting apart. In zone 9 it is planted in fall and harvested November through February, with color and flavor improving with each frost.
sources: Slow Food Foundation — Radicchio Rosso di Treviso Precoce
Seeds of Italy — Rossa di Treviso Growing Notes
University of California Cooperative Extension — Radicchio and Chicory Varieties

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before fall transplant; in zone 9 transplant September–October for a November–February harvest. Sugar Loaf chicory is considerably milder and less bitter than radicchio or Belgian endive — the name 'Pain de Sucre' (bread of sugar) reflects its unusual sweetness for a chicory. In zone 9 it produces consistently through winter without protection and is harvested before spring warmth causes the plant to bolt.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Sugar Loaf Chicory (Pain de Sucre) is a French chicory producing tall, upright, cylindrical heads similar in shape to a Belgian endive or small romaine — but green throughout rather than blanched white. The name reflects the characteristic sweetness of this chicory type: the tightly-wrapped leaves are significantly milder and less bitter than radicchio or loose-leaf chicories, making Sugar Loaf accessible to people who find standard chicory too bitter for raw salad use. The crisp, substantial leaves with a mild, slightly bitter, refreshing flavor are excellent raw in salads, braised, or used as a sturdy wrap for composed appetizers. Sugar Loaf tolerates storage better than most lettuces — wrapped heads keep 2–3 weeks in the refrigerator, providing fresh salad greens long after harvest. In zone 9 the fall-through-winter growing season produces excellent Sugar Loaf chicory from October plantings harvested through February, when mild winters allow unprotected outdoor overwintering.
sources: Seeds of Italy — Pain de Sucre Sugar Loaf Chicory Growing Notes
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Chicory and Radicchio
University of California Cooperative Extension — Chicory Production Guide
